description: AAPL COVID pandemic drop. Mentor's canonical test case - everyone knows what caused this, which lets us spot-check whether the pipeline produces sensible attributions. DO NOT change these inputs while iterating prompts. Freeze the test case so you can isolate which prompt change caused which output change.
mentor_quote: Apple in March 2020 is a good example because everyone knows COVID caused the drop. Picking events you already understand lets you spot-check whether the system is producing sensible attributions. Without that intuition baseline, you're flying blind when you iterate on prompts.

input.context: Monday after Fed's Sunday emergency rate cut to 0 and national emergency declared. Broad COVID panic. S&P 500 -12%, VIX ~80.

non_dominant_dimensions_acceptable.demand: negative or neutral (expected demand drop from lockdowns is fine)
non_dominant_dimensions_acceptable.pricing: neutral (nothing AAPL-specific on pricing that day)
non_dominant_dimensions_acceptable.management_credibility: neutral (no AAPL guidance change that day)

fade_or_follow_expectation.reason: Move was overwhelmingly macro-driven panic; market recovered within months. Move character should be 'transient'. If model predicts -5% but realized -13%, that's overreaction -> fade.

failure_modes_to_catch: Model cites AAPL product news or guidance as dominant driver for a move that was overwhelmingly macro.
Model cites evidence chunks with publication_date > 2020-03-16 - foreknowledge leak.
predicted_return_pct comes back positive (the direction is unambiguous).
Coherence check passes despite nonsense attribution (e.g. 'crude oil moved AAPL').
No MACRO-type chunks in evidence_chunk_ids once +macro ablation is included - we're not using the data we fetched.
